fullDescription.text: User-controlled input is passed directly into a MongoDB query without sanitization, allowing an attacker to inject query operators such as $gt, $ne, or $regex to manipulate query logic.

shortDescription.text: Broken Authentication - Plaintext password storage and comparison

fullDescription.text: Passwords are stored and compared in plaintext instead of using a secure hashing algorithm such as bcrypt. This allows credential theft if the database is compromised.
User-controlled input from query string is used directly in a redirect without validation, allowing an attacker to redirect users to malicious sites.

message.text: The 'userName' parameter from the login request is passed directly into MongoDB's findOne() query without sanitization. An attacker can submit a JSON object such as {"$gt": ""} as the userName field, causing the query to match unintended documents and bypass authentication.

The 'password' parameter in validateLogin is not type-checked or sanitized before being compared. When combined with NoSQL injection on the userName field, an attacker can pass an operator object as the password (e.g., {"$gt": ""}) that evaluates to true in the plaintext comparison, fully bypassing authentication.
Passwords are stored in plaintext in MongoDB. The bcrypt hashing code in addUser (line 29) is commented out. If the database is compromised, all user credentials are immediately exposed.

Password comparison uses strict equality (===) on plaintext values instead of bcrypt.compareSync(). The secure comparison code is commented out at line 65. This, combined with plaintext storage, means authentication offers no defense against database credential leaks.

The /learn route uses req.query.url directly in res.redirect() without any validation or allowlist check. An attacker can craft a link such as /learn?url=https://evil.com to redirect authenticated users to a phishing or malware site.
